pattern_gallery.label: Royal Fork — knight forks king and queen
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's knight on c7 simultaneously attacks the Black king on e8 and the Black queen on a8. Black must move the king out of check, losing the queen on the next move.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The Royal Fork (knight attacks K + Q at the same time) is the most devastating tactical pattern in chess. Watch for any geometry where your knight could land on a square attacking both.

pattern_gallery.label: Pawn Fork — d2-d4 attacks rook AND bishop
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays d4! The pawn now attacks Black's rook on c5 AND Black's bishop on e5 simultaneously. Black can only save one. Pawn forks are the cheapest possible attacker.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Before every pawn move, ask: does this pawn attack two enemy pieces on the diagonal squares ahead? Pawns capture diagonally — a single pawn push can win material.

pattern_gallery.label: Two Knights / Fried Liver setup — Ng5 threatens fork
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4 Nf6 4.Ng5, White's knight on g5 attacks f7 — a square defended only by Black's king. Next move Nxf7 attacks the queen on d8 AND the rook on h8 (a family fork). Black must respond immediately (...d5 is the main line).
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The f7/f2 square is the fork incubator in EVERY king-pawn opening. Any time an enemy knight reaches g5/g4 with a bishop eyeing f7/f2, calculate the Nxf7 fork before doing anything else.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Nd6+ — family fork of king and rook
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Nd6+! The knight jumps to d6 attacking the Black king on e8 AND the rook on c8 at the same time. Black must move the king out of check, then White plays Nxc8 winning the rook.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Knights on the 6th rank are forking machines. Squares c6/d6/e6/f6 are the classic outpost fork squares — when a knight lands there with check, scan for what other piece it also attacks.

pattern_gallery.label: Queen fork — c-file check + 6th rank attack
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's queen on c6 checks the Black king on c8 (c-file) AND simultaneously attacks the rook on h6 (6th rank). The queen operates on two lines at once. Black must move the king, then White plays Qxh6 winning the rook.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The queen is the only piece that can fork along ANY two intersecting lines (file + rank, rank + diagonal, two diagonals). Whenever your queen reaches a square with check, look down every line it controls for a second target.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Nxf7 — royal-fork variant K + Q
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Nf7+! The knight on h6 jumps to f7, attacking the Black king on h8 AND the Black queen on d8. Black must move the king (only legal move), and White captures the queen with Nxd8.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The knight is the king's worst nightmare — it can attack 8 squares at once from a single perch, with no possibility of blocking. When the enemy king is cornered, every knight move within 2 squares is a fork candidate.

pattern_gallery.label: Find e5 — pawn forks knight and bishop
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays e5! The pawn lurches forward attacking the Black knight on d6 AND the Black bishop on f6 at once (diagonal pawn captures). Black can only save one piece — the other is lost for the cost of a single pawn.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Pawn forks are the highest-leverage tactics in chess: 1 point of material attacking 6 points (N+B). Before every pawn push, ask: does this pawn arrive on a square diagonally attacking two enemy pieces?

pattern_gallery.label: Italian Game — White's Bg5 pin on the f6 knight
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4 Nf6 4.d3 Bc5 5.Bg5, White's bishop pins Black's knight on f6 to the queen on d8. The knight is functionally frozen — moving it costs the queen.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Relative pins on knights to queens are everywhere in classical openings. Defenses: ...h6 (kick the bishop), ...d6 (preserve the pin geometry), ...Be7 (block the pin).

pattern_gallery.label: Absolute pin — queen frozen on the e-file
pattern_gallery.explanation: Black's queen is pinned to the king by White's rook on the e-file. ABSOLUTE PIN — Black cannot legally move the queen (would expose the king to check).
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Recognize this geometry instantly. King + valuable piece + opposing rook (or queen, or bishop on diagonal) on the same line = absolute pin. This is why castling early and making a luft matter.

pattern_gallery.label: Ruy Lopez — Bb5 pin on the c6 knight
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bb5, White's bishop pins the knight on c6 to the king on e8 along the b5-e8 diagonal. The knight cannot legally move — doing so exposes the king to check (absolute pin geometry, though the bishop pin is technically relative because ...a6/...b5 can unwind it).
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The Ruy Lopez pin is the most-played pin in chess history. Black's main defenses: ...a6 (challenge the bishop), ...Nf6 (develop and prepare ...d6), ...Nge7 (avoid the pin entirely). The pin's purpose is to weaken Black's grip on e5.

pattern_gallery.label: Queen's Gambit — Bg5 pin on the f6 knight
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.d4 d5 2.c4 e6 3.Nc3 Nf6 4.Bg5, White's bishop pins the knight on f6 to the queen on d8. Moving the knight loses the queen. This is the central pin of every classical Queen's Gambit Declined.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Bg5 pinning Nf6 to the queen is identical geometry to Bg4 pinning Nf3 to the queen — both sides use it. Defenses include ...Be7 (block and prepare to break), ...h6 (kick the bishop), or accepting the pin and playing around it.

pattern_gallery.label: Absolute pin — knight frozen on the f-file
pattern_gallery.explanation: Black's knight on f7 is pinned to the king on f8 by White's rook on f1. The knight CANNOT legally move — any move exposes the king to check from the rook. White will simply pile up attackers on f7 to win the knight.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Absolute pins (to the king) make the pinned piece worth zero — it can defend, but it cannot capture or move. Pile attackers on the pinned piece (with pawns or minor pieces) until you have more attackers than defenders, then win the material.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Rxd4 — pinned queen wins material
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Rxd4! Black's queen on d4 is pinned to the rook on d8 by White's doubled rooks on the d-file. After Rxd4, if Black recaptures with Rxd4, White plays Rxd4 again — net: White wins queen for rook (a clear material gain of 4 points).
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Doubled rooks on an open file create deadly pins. When you see a major piece on an open file with another piece of its color behind it, you have a pin to exploit — capture the front piece, the back one cannot resist.

pattern_gallery.label: Bishop pin on diagonal — knight pinned to queen
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's bishop on b5 pins the Black knight on c6 to the queen on e8 along the b5-e8 diagonal. The knight is functionally frozen (relative pin — moving it costs the queen for a bishop). Black must lose tempo defending or unpinning.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Diagonal pins from a bishop are everywhere. Scan every enemy long-diagonal piece for what sits behind your pieces along that diagonal. The cure: block the diagonal with a pawn, or move the back piece off the line.

pattern_gallery.label: Bishop Skewer — win the queen
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Bf6+ (or any bishop check on the long diagonal). The Black king on d3 must move out of check. Once the king moves, the bishop captures the queen on h1 behind it.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Skewer = pin in reverse. Higher-value piece in front (king, must move), lower-value behind (queen, gets captured). Long-range pieces (bishop, rook, queen) on long lines = skewer territory.

pattern_gallery.label: Rook Skewer — endgame win
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Rh1+ — wait, the rook is already on h1. White's rook attacks Black's king on d1. Black king must move, exposing Black's rook on a1 to capture.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Rook skewers along ranks and files dominate endgames. King exposed on the back rank = vulnerability. Always look for skewer geometry in simplified positions.

pattern_gallery.label: Bishop skewer — king in front, queen behind
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's bishop on e4 (defended by the king on e3) checks Black's king on f5. The Black queen on g6 sits behind the king on the same diagonal e4-f5-g6. Black must move the king — then Bxg6 wins the queen.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Skewer geometry = attacker + higher-value piece (king/queen) + lower-value piece, all on one line. The king MUST move (legal obligation), exposing the piece behind. Look for skewers any time you see two enemy pieces aligned with king closer.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Ra8+ — rook skewer on the 8th rank
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Ra8+! The rook reaches the 8th rank with check on the king at e8. Black must move the king (or block with Qb8 — but then Rxb8+ still wins the queen). After the king moves to e7 or similar, Rxh8 wins the queen.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Rook skewers along ranks dominate exposed-king endgames. When the enemy king and a heavy piece sit on the same rank/file with empty squares between, look for ways to deliver check on that line.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Bd4+ — bishop skewer on the long diagonal
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Bd4+! The bishop slides to d4 attacking the Black king on e5. Behind the king on the same a1-h8 diagonal sits the queen on h8. Black must move the king, then Bxh8 wins the queen.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The a1-h8 and h1-a8 long diagonals are skewer highways. Any time enemy king + queen sit on the same long diagonal, calculate whether your bishop can land on a square checking the king with the queen behind.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Qh2+ — queen diagonal skewer
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Qh2+! The queen slides to h2 attacking the king on e5 along the h2-e5 diagonal. Behind the king on the same diagonal sits the Black rook on b8. Black king must move, then Qxb8 wins the rook.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The queen can skewer on ANY of the 8 directions a queen moves. Before any queen move, ask: is this move (or any queen move) putting two enemy pieces on one of my queen's lines with the king closer?

pattern_gallery.label: Find Rh3+ — rook skewer on the h-file
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Rh3+! The rook slides from a3 to h3, checking the Black king on h4 along the h-file. Behind the king on the same file sits the Black rook on h7. Black king must move sideways, then Rxh7 wins the rook even-trade-plus-tempo.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Rook skewers along files are common in middlegames where one side has exposed their king. Watch your rook's lines — every rook move is a candidate skewer if two enemy pieces line up with the king closer.

pattern_gallery.label: Discovered Attack — bishop moves, queen attacks
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's bishop on g5 is in front of the queen's diagonal to d5 (Black's queen). If the bishop moves anywhere safely (e.g. Bxf6), it reveals the queen's attack on Black's queen. Discovered attack wins material.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Stack pieces on a line (bishop + queen on a diagonal, rook + knight on a file). Move the front piece for free → reveal the attack from behind. Most powerful when the moving piece ALSO creates its own threat.

pattern_gallery.label: Légal's Mate setup — discovered idea (pre-sacrifice)
pattern_gallery.explanation: After ...Bxd1?? in Légal's trap. White has just played Bxf7+. Now after King moves to e7, Nd5 is checkmate — knight check + discovered mate from bishop. The most famous discovered-attack-into-mate pattern.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Discovered checks are forcing. Combined with a mate threat from the moving piece's destination = forced sequence. Memorize Légal's Mate (1750!) as the canonical pattern.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Nf5+ — discovered attack on the queen
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Nf5+! The knight on h4 jumps to f5 delivering check to the king on e7 — AND the moving knight uncovers the rook on h1's attack on the queen on h8 (the h-file opens). Black must address the check; after the king moves, Rxh8 wins the queen.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Discovered attacks deliver TWO threats from ONE move — the moving piece's attack PLUS the unmasked attack from the piece behind it. When the moving piece also gives check, the discovery is forced — the opponent cannot ignore it.

pattern_gallery.label: Discovered-check battery — bishop behind knight on a1-h8
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's bishop on a1 and knight on d4 form a discovered-check battery on the a1-h8 long diagonal aimed at the Black king on h8. If the knight moves ANYWHERE, the bishop delivers check. Combined with a useful knight move (a fork, a capture, a threat), this is devastating.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Build discovered-check batteries deliberately. The moving piece is essentially free to grab whatever it wants because the check from behind FORCES the opponent to address the king. Always scan for batteries on the long diagonals when your king is exposed.

pattern_gallery.label: Find a bishop move — discovered attack on the queen
pattern_gallery.explanation: Black's bishop on d6 blocks the Black queen on d8 from attacking the White queen on d5 down the d-file. ANY bishop move (e.g. ...Bb4+ checking and unmasking, or ...Bf4 attacking) reveals the queen attack. Best is ...Bb4+ — check AND discovered attack on the queen — winning the queen.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Stack your pieces on a line (Q behind B, R behind N) to create discovery potential. The moving piece becomes 'free' — opponent has no time to deal with both threats.

pattern_gallery.label: Battery setup — rook behind bishop on d-file
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's rook on d3, bishop on d5, and Black queen on d8 are all on the d-file. The bishop currently blocks the rook's attack on the queen. If the bishop moves anywhere with tempo (a check, a fork, a threat), the rook's attack on the queen is revealed and the queen is lost.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: A piece in front of a heavier piece of the same color is a LATENT discovered attack. Always look for moves where the front piece relocates with a threat — the back piece does the real damage for free.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Bxh6+ — discovered check captures the queen
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Bxh6+! The bishop on e3 slides to h6 capturing the queen, AND simultaneously uncovers a discovered check on the king on e8 from the rook on e1. Black must respond to check — White has captured the queen for free.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Discovered check + capture = the most devastating combination in chess. The opponent CANNOT recapture because they must address the check first. This is why discovered checks are sometimes called 'the windmill's older brother.'

pattern_gallery.label: Classic Back-Rank Mate — rook delivers
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Re8#. The Black king on g8 is trapped by its own pawns on f7, g7, h7 — no escape squares. Rook on the 8th rank delivers checkmate. The defining mate pattern of millions of games.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: After both sides castle, the back rank becomes vulnerable. Defense: make a luft (push h6/h3 for an escape square). Offense: scan for opponent's blocked king every move with major pieces.

pattern_gallery.label: Back-Rank with defender — sacrifice to remove
pattern_gallery.explanation: Black's rook on a8 defends the 8th rank. White plays Re8+! Rxe8 (forced — king has no moves), but White has set up the position for a follow-up battery in real games. Setup demonstrates removing the defender via trade.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: When the back rank has ONE defender, look for ways to remove it (capture, deflect, pin). Removing the defender = back rank becomes mateable.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Re8# — doubled-rook back-rank mate
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Re8#. The rook delivers check on the 8th rank; the Black king on g8 is trapped by its own pawns on f7/g7/h7 with no escape squares (f8 and h8 are also covered by the rook on the 8th rank). The second white rook on a1 is just insurance.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: After both sides castle without making luft, the back rank is the #1 mate threat in the game. ANY enemy major piece reaching your back rank can be mate. Push h3/h6 early if you sense back-rank danger.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Qe8# — queen back-rank mate
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Qe8#. The queen delivers check on the 8th rank with no possibility of capture (Black king on g8 cannot reach e8). The king has no escape — f7/g7/h7 are blocked by its own pawns, f8/h8 are covered by the queen along the 8th rank.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The queen mates on the back rank just like a rook does — and from MORE squares because of its diagonal reach. When you see an unprotected back rank with a queen anywhere near it, you have a candidate mate.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Re8+ Rxe8 Qxe8# — sacrifice + mate
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Re8+! Black must capture Rxe8 (forced — only the rook defends the back rank, and no piece can interpose or block). Then Qxe8# — the queen recaptures and delivers mate on the back rank. Black king has no escape (pawns block f7/g7/h7, queen covers f8/h8).
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: When a single piece defends the back rank, REMOVE IT by force — sacrifice, deflect, overload, or trade. The classic pattern: rook sac to draw the defender, then queen mate. Memorize this sequence.

pattern_gallery.label: Luft defense — h6 saves the king
pattern_gallery.explanation: Black has pushed h7-h6 making luft (an escape square for the king on h7). White plays Re8+, but unlike the standard back-rank mate, the Black king simply runs to h7 — no mate, equal game. The single pawn move on move 1-15 prevented a future disaster.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Push h6 (or h3 as White) the moment your king is castled and major pieces are still on the board — it costs one tempo and saves the game. The pawn move is called LUFT (German for 'air'). Make it a habit before move 20.

pattern_gallery.label: Mutual back-rank vulnerability — whoever moves first wins
pattern_gallery.explanation: Both kings have no luft (no escape squares on the 7th rank — pawns block them all). Both sides have a rook on the back rank. Whoever delivers check first wins — here Black plays Ra1+ and there's no defense (White king on g1 has no escape, no piece to block).
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: In symmetric endgames with both back ranks weak, TEMPO is everything. The side to move wins. Either deliver the back-rank check first OR make luft first — but not both, choose.

pattern_gallery.label: King + Queen Mate — knight opposition delivers
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Qg7# (or Qh7#). The White king on g6 supports the queen, the Black king on h8 has no escape. Classic K+Q mate where the queen lands on a square defended by the king, attacking the cornered king.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: K+Q mate technique: use knight opposition to box the king into a corner, leave 2 squares for shuffling, march your king up to support, deliver the ONE check that ends it. Never give early checks.

pattern_gallery.label: King + Rook Mate — direct opposition delivers
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Ra8#. The White king on g6 covers g7 and h7 (Black king's only potential escape squares). The rook delivers mate on the 8th rank. The kings are in direct opposition, then rook check = mate.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: K+R mate technique: rook cuts a file/rank, your king uses direct opposition to push enemy king to the edge, then rook check = mate. Use waiting moves (slide rook on the cut file) to force enemy king into opposition.

pattern_gallery.label: K+Q technique start — box and march
pattern_gallery.explanation: White has K+Q vs lone Black king. The queen on d6 already restricts the Black king to the 8th rank (king cannot cross the 6th-rank barrier). The technique now: bring the White king up, maintain the box, then deliver mate on the edge with king support. Do NOT give early checks — they waste time and risk stalemate.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: K+Q vs K mating recipe: (1) Use the queen to restrict enemy king to a smaller area (knight-distance away from enemy king works perfectly). (2) March your king up to support. (3) Mate on the edge with one decisive check, defended by your king.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Qg7# — K+Q corner mate
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Qg7#! The queen moves to g7 attacking the Black king on h8 (diagonal g7-h8). The Black king cannot capture the queen — it's defended by the White king on f8. The king has no other moves (h7 and g8 are also covered by the queen, and h8 itself is attacked).
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The cleanest K+Q mate: enemy king in the corner, your queen one square diagonal away (the 'queen kiss'), your king defending. Memorize this geometric end-state — every K+Q endgame should aim for it.

pattern_gallery.label: Find Rh8# — K+R edge mate via opposition
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Rh8#! The rook reaches the 8th rank delivering check to the king on b8. White's king on b6 takes opposition with the Black king, controlling all escape squares (a7, b7, c7 all attacked by the White king; a8 and c8 covered by the rook on h8). Mate.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: K+R vs K mating recipe: rook cuts the enemy king off on a rank/file, your king takes DIRECT OPPOSITION on the file the enemy king is trapped on (2 squares away, kings on same file), then rook check on the 8th rank = mate.

pattern_gallery.label: K+R technique — opposition + waiting move
pattern_gallery.explanation: White K on d3 has direct opposition with Black K on d5. The rook on a1 is ready to deliver check along the 5th rank or to slide horizontally as a waiting move. Plays like Ra5+! drive the king back rank by rank. This is the BOX-AND-LADDER technique mid-execution.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: When you have K+R vs K, use the rook to CUT OFF a rank/file the enemy king cannot cross. Your king takes opposition. When the enemy king zigzags, the rook waits (slide along the cut file) until king is forced into opposition again. Then the rook check drops a rank — repeat until mate.

pattern_gallery.label: STALEMATE TRAP — the cardinal K+Q sin
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's queen on g6 and king on f6 have left the Black king on h8 with no legal moves AND no check. RESULT: stalemate — instant draw despite White's overwhelming material advantage. This is the most common K+Q vs K disaster.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: In K+Q endgames, ALWAYS leave the enemy king at least one move (or deliver check). NEVER put your queen on a square that takes away the king's only square without checking. The safe technique: keep queen a knight-move away from the king while marching your own king up.

pattern_gallery.label: Direct Opposition — kings face one square apart
pattern_gallery.explanation: Kings on d3 and d5 face each other one square apart, with White's pawn on d2. WHITE TO MOVE means Black has the opposition — White's king must yield (move sideways), Black king can advance to the key squares c4/d4/e4 to block the pawn.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The player NOT to move has the opposition. In K+P endings, this determines who can promote. Count parity carefully — sometimes triangulate to switch who has the move.

pattern_gallery.label: Outflanking — king escorts the pawn
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's king on e6 is in front of the pawn AND has the opposition (Black to move after White's first move). The textbook winning K+P endgame: king clears the path, pawn promotes.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Rule for K+P vs K: if the attacking king is IN FRONT of its pawn AND has the opposition, the pawn promotes. Memorize this — it converts countless endgames.

pattern_gallery.label: Distant opposition — kings 6 squares apart
pattern_gallery.explanation: Two lone kings on the e-file, 6 squares apart. White to move. The side NOT to move has the opposition — here Black has the opposition because White must yield first. In a real K+P endgame, this kind of parity calculation determines who promotes.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Opposition rule: when kings face each other on the same file/rank/diagonal with an EVEN number of empty squares between them, the side NOT to move has the opposition. Count carefully — direct (1 square), distant (3, 5, 7 squares).

pattern_gallery.label: Rule of the Square — Black king can catch the pawn
pattern_gallery.explanation: White pawn on a2 needs to promote on a8 (6 squares). Black king on e5 is INSIDE the square (the 6x6 area from a2-a8-f8-f3 — verify: e5 is inside since max(|e-a|, |5-8|) = 4 ≤ 6 moves available). Black catches the pawn — draw.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Rule of the Square: draw an imaginary square from the pawn to its promotion rank. If the defending king is inside that square (or can step in on its move), the king catches the pawn. This calculation takes 2 seconds and decides countless endgames.

pattern_gallery.label: Key squares occupied — White wins regardless of opposition
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's king on d6 occupies a KEY SQUARE for the d-pawn (the squares c6/d6/e6 are the key squares for a d-pawn before it reaches the 5th rank). When the attacking king reaches a key square, the position is winning REGARDLESS of opposition — the pawn promotes by force.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Key squares > opposition. For pawns on the 2nd-5th rank: key squares are the 3 squares two ranks ahead of the pawn. For 6th-rank pawns: key squares are the 3 squares directly ahead. If your king occupies a key square, the pawn wins.

pattern_gallery.label: Rook-pawn draw — the corner is a haven
pattern_gallery.explanation: A rook pawn (a-pawn or h-pawn) often cannot win even with the king escorting it. Here Black plays Kxa3 (capturing the pawn) — draw. Even without that, Black can shuffle Ka4-Kb4 and White's king cannot make progress without abandoning the pawn.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Rook pawns are SPECIAL — the defending king can shoulder against the wall and force stalemate or king-trade. If you're ahead with a rook pawn and the enemy king reaches the corner, it's a draw. Convert before this happens, or avoid rook-pawn endings.

pattern_gallery.label: K+P endgame — who has the opposition?
pattern_gallery.explanation: White K on d2 with pawn on d3, Black K on d5. Black to move. Kings face each other 3 squares apart on the d-file (2 empty squares between). Black must move — yielding the opposition to White. White advances Kd3? No — Kd2 to e3 sideways, gaining ground. The pawn ending is winning for White only with correct opposition play.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: In K+P endgames, the king MUST lead the pawn (not push the pawn first). Get the king in front of the pawn with opposition. If you push the pawn too early, you lose the opposition battle and the game draws.

pattern_gallery.label: Three Principles in action — Center + Develop
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3, White has placed a pawn in the center (Center ✓) and developed a knight toward the center attacking e5 (Develop ✓). Black has the same. Both are following the opening principles.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Every good opening implements: (1) Center — control with pawns, (2) Develop — knights and bishops out, (3) Castle — king to safety. Pieces in the center see more (knight in center: 8 squares; in corner: 2).

pattern_gallery.label: Reti / KID setup — flexible center principle
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.d4 Nf6 2.Nf3, both sides have developed knights toward the center without committing the e/c pawns. Modern flexible opening showing 'develop first, commit later' philosophy.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Opening principles are flexible. You don't have to push BOTH central pawns immediately — sometimes 'control' the center via pieces (hypermodern). The principle is intent, not literal pawn placement.

pattern_gallery.label: Knights before bishops — 1.Nc3 in action
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.Nc3, White has developed a knight first. The principle 'knights before bishops' exists because knights have clear best squares (c3/f3/c6/f6) while bishop development depends on pawn structure decisions you haven't made yet. Develop the obvious pieces first.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Knights belong on c3/f3 (or c6/f6) in 99% of openings — develop them without thinking. Bishops require a pawn move first to clear their diagonal, and their best square varies by opening. Knights first = no commitment errors.

pattern_gallery.label: Italian Game — both sides castled, all principles met
pattern_gallery.explanation: A textbook Italian Game position: both sides have controlled the center (e4/e5), developed knights to f3/f6 and c3 will follow, developed bishops to c4/c5, and castled kingside. Every opening principle has been satisfied by move 6 — this is what good opening play looks like.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: By move 8-10, you should have: pawns in the center, both knights developed, at least one bishop developed, and your king castled. If you check off all 4 by move 10, you've followed the principles correctly — you're now ready to make a middlegame plan.

pattern_gallery.label: Don't move pieces twice — Scandinavian queen kicked
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.e4 d5 2.exd5 Qxd5 3.Nc3, Black has used 2 of 3 opening moves on the queen and now must move it a THIRD time (attacked by the knight on c3). White develops with tempo while Black wastes moves shuffling the queen. This is the cost of early queen play.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: DON'T bring your queen out before your minor pieces. The queen is so valuable that opponents can develop their minor pieces WITH TEMPO by attacking her. Every queen move that gets attacked = a wasted move + a free development move for your opponent.

pattern_gallery.label: Center control — Queen's Gambit (1.d4 d5 2.c4)
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.d4 d5 2.c4, White challenges Black's central pawn with the famous Queen's Gambit. The c4 pawn is offered to undermine Black's d5 stronghold and seize central control. This is THE classical fight for the center in d-pawn openings.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The center is the most valuable real estate on the board. Pieces in the center control more squares (a knight on e5 attacks 8 squares; on a1 it attacks 2). Fight for the center on every move 1 of the opening — push d4/e4 (or d5/e5) and back the pawns with pieces.

pattern_gallery.label: Wayward queen — 2.Qg4? violates every principle
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.e4 e5 2.Qg4?, White has developed the queen ON MOVE TWO before any minor piece. Black gets a free attack with 2...Nc6 (developing AND attacking the queen). White will waste 2-3 moves shuffling the queen while Black develops freely — Black has a near-winning position by move 8.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Punishing early queen sorties is automatic: respond by developing pieces that ATTACK the queen. Every tempo your opponent loses moving the queen is a free move for you. This is why 'develop knights before queen' is sacred.

openings_library.name: London System
openings_library.summary: Universal d4 system. Bishop comes out to f4 BEFORE getting blocked. Same plan against almost anything Black plays — Levy's #1 pick for sub-1500.

openings_library.name: Vienna System
openings_library.summary: Levy's e4 weapon — 70% personal win rate. Sets up the Vienna Gambit (3.f4) against ...Nf6. Off the beaten path; opponents rarely know it.

openings_library.name: Alapin Sicilian (anti-Sicilian for White)
openings_library.summary: Sidesteps 90% of Sicilian theory by playing 2.c3 instead of 2.Nf3. Prepares d4. Removes opponent's prep advantage.

openings_library.name: Italian Game
openings_library.summary: Classical e4 opening, named after 17th-c. Italian master Greco. Bishop aims at f7 (Black's weakest square). Solid, principled.

openings_library.name: Ruy López (Spanish Opening)
openings_library.summary: The most-studied opening at master level. Bishop attacks the c6 knight defending the e5 pawn. Carlsen's go-to — but theory-heavy.

openings_library.name: Queen's Gambit
openings_library.summary: Classic d4 opening offering the c-pawn. Black usually declines (QGD). Got mainstream attention via the Netflix series.

openings_library.name: Caro-Kann Defense
openings_library.summary: Levy's PERSONAL favorite — beat 100+ GMs with it. Building-block defense (c6 supports d5). Solid pawn structure. Drops White's win rate from 51% to 47% at sub-1600.

openings_library.name: Scandinavian Defense
openings_library.summary: Force White to react on move 1. Queen out early (the exception that proves the rule). Memorize: Qd5 → Qa5 → c6 (queen escape).

openings_library.name: Sicilian Defense
openings_library.summary: Most popular Black response at GM level. Asymmetric counter-attack. Theory-dense (Najdorf, Dragon, Sveshnikov, etc.) — Levy doesn't recommend for sub-1500.

openings_library.name: French Defense
openings_library.summary: Building-block sister to Caro-Kann (e6 supports d5 instead of c6). Creates closed positions. Light-squared bishop is the problem child.

openings_library.name: Queen's Gambit Declined (setup)
openings_library.summary: Levy's pick vs d4 (and other slow openings). Decline the c4 pawn — accepting bumps White's win rate to 56%. Setup transposes to London, English, Reti.

openings_library.name: King's Indian Defense (KID)
openings_library.summary: Setup-based defense playable against most White openings. Kasparov's favorite. Counterpunching style — closed positions, kingside pawn storms.
